TRANSCRIPT
Okay, so this section of the Secrets of Leverage and Scale is all about the big
picture. This is the advanced stuff. This is how to get the life that you want.
So, the first thing I want to talk about is what I consider probably the most
important thing in business and that is opportunity cost. Opportunity cost is the cost that
you will have when you take on a project. What will taking on that project prevent you
from doing? And often in pure business world, the opportunity cost to start up is a
financial thing. Like if you spend this million dollars to create this project, what projects
do you have to forego that you could have created with that million dollars? So, I go and I
spend this million dollars to create this great restaurant -- what else could I have done
with that million dollars? But in our kinds of businesses, which are generally not as
capital intensive but more time intensive, the same thing applies, in fact, more so because
time is more limited than financial resources.
If you take this project on, what project will you not be able to take on? What
project are you giving up by taking this project on? If you take this client on, what other
clients are you going to have to say no to? There's this quote I love by Warren Buffett who
is probably the most successful investor ever. He's a billionaire many, many times over,
and he says, “The difference between successful people and really successful people is that
really successful people say no to almost everything.” Now, saying no doesn't come easy to
very many of us -- to most of us, we have a hard time saying no. But that's the thing, you
always have to say, "What is the opportunity cost here?"
My friend Dean Graziosi ... He's sitting in his office and behind him is a bookcase of
his favorite books. He just loves these books. He cherishes these books -- and the
bookcase is completely full. Someone comes in and they have just a wonderful book ... or
a book they're trying to sell him or they're trying to give him or they're trying to get him to
consume. And he looks at that and says, "If I take this book, I'm gonna have to turn
around to my bookcase and I'm gonna have to take one of those books and throw it away,"
and of course the books are opportunities. So, if someone comes in and says, "Here I've
got this great opportunity for you. I've got this great project that you can take on. I need
your help with this project," then he has to take a look at all his other projects, and
potential projects, and throw one of those away. Or he has to say no.
That's why saying no is so critical, because the number of opportunities that come
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
your way ... there's no end to the number of opportunities that come your way. And as you
become more successful in business then your deal flow, the number of possible deals
that are coming to you, will skyrocket. So opportunity cost, in my opinion, the most
important thing in business. And as your business grows, it will become more important.
The pressures on your time become greater. The potential for your time to become
fractured become greater. More people want a piece of you. They keep on bringing more
things in front of you -- your time is more valuable in a very direct financial sense. That's
the way it works.
As you become more successful, you're also getting older and time's moving faster
and is ever more fleeting, so you have to get really good at saying no. I know I can
remember -- this was a long time ago -- when my income was a fraction of what it is today
and at some point I said, "You know, any new project comes along, it has to bring at least
50,000 dollars to me," and this was way early days, "This has to bring 50,000 dollars to
me, like almost assuredly, before I even start to think about it." And that was back when I
was a lot more financially motivated than I am now. So you have to say no. It is a critical
skill for you to develop is the ability to say no, and that ability ... It just become more
important as your business grows because the deal flow, the amount of possible deals
coming in will just explode as you become more successful.
One of the things that you might consider is focusing on what's the value of a "focus
day.” So a focus day is something I learned from Dan Sullivan he tries to set up his days
so that he has free days where he's not working on anything -- anything business related -
- and then he also has "buffer days", and that's where he does sort of the ... gets stuck
doing the nitty-gritty, so to speak, of the business ... the pain in the neck stuff that all of us
seem stuck doing -- you know, the filing, and the taxes, and administrative stuff. And
hopefully as your business grows and your team grows, you do less and less of that, but
it's hard to completely eliminate it 100%. So, he has free days and "buffer days" -- and
hopefully there aren't very many buffer days -- but then there's also "focus days" and
that's where you're doing your genius work. This is all Dan Sullivan 101.
One of the things that's valuable is to calculate what's the value of a focus day
because the reality is you don't have that many focus days in a year. If you can get 100
focus days in a year, or 150 focus days would be pretty heroic, in my opinion. Let's just say
you get somewhere between 100, 150 focus days a year. How much is one of those days
worth? These are the days where you're just ... you don't have any distractions and you're
purely doing your genius work -- for me that's creating marketing, it's creating content,
creating training, like I'm doing right now, or it's building relationships with my partners.

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
So those are like the three primary focus day activities I've got. And what is the value of an
entire day spent on those focus activities? Those “make more” activities?
There’s lots of different ways that you can calculate this, but if you just sit down
and say, "I have a hundred of those days a year, and those are the days that drive the
money, so those are the money making days. This is how much money I make in a year,"
and then you simply divide it by how many focus days you get in a year, and you can start
to get a picture of what is a focus day worth. And it's probably worth a lot more than you
think because let's just that you take weekends off. Okay, so you take weekends off, so
that's... Well, there's 52 weekends in a year, so that's 104 days. So you're down to about
260 days in a year, right?
Then let's say you take off four weeks a year. Now, as an entrepreneur, you might
be an overworked entrepreneur and you might not take off any days -- you might not even
take off weekends. You might work 365 days a year -- I hope you don't, but there are some
people like that. But let's say you take out weekends and four weeks a year, that takes you
onto 150 days ... I think it does. No, it takes you down to 250 ... I shouldn't be doing math
here while I'm recording. But let's say that takes you ... the weekends would take you
down to 250 days a year, take off 20 days, you're down to 230 days a year, maybe 80 of
those days are buffer days so now you're down to 150 days a year. So, take whatever your
income is and divide it by 150 and then you get the value of a focus day. You can even
drop that down and look at what the value of a focus hour is. Now, you can start to
understand how important those days are.
Or maybe you've got a job and so you're working that job, so that makes your time
and your business even much, much, much shorter, and now you can start to say ... you
might only have 20 focus days a year. But it's important for you to just to quantify what a
focus day is worth and maybe even what a focus hour is worth because that will help you
guide you ... that will help guide you in your decision making and you can start to say ... It
might get a little easier to say no to those projects that are just not going to create the
income and the lifestyle that you want.
Another thing to focus on is how much are you making? When we're just talking
about dollars, how much are you ... A lot of times, especially when you're around a bunch
of marketers, you'll hear them talking about sales -- the sales dollars. But it's important to
track your sales profit -- look at what your profit is. Now, there are times where sales can
be really important, sometimes where the sales are more important than others maybe for
positioning, or for market power. These days, if you look at something like Amazon where

they're more about sales than they are about profits. There's a lot of the newer tech
companies that are like that and they're trying to amass this huge market share and this
huge market positioning, but in our kinds of businesses it usually pays to focus on the
profit than the bottom-line. So remember, sales is sort of a vanity, often it's a vanity
number, but the profit's the important number. And so always be looking at your projects
and what you're taking on with the idea of not just of sales, but also profit. And I know
that sounds like a really simple thing, but a lot of people screw that up.
And then the number ... Throughout all of this, you really just have to focus on
what do you want in your life? I mean, sit down and define what do you want in your life?
What do you value in your life? I am driven to make an impact, to help people out, but I'm
also driven to have a great life, great lifestyle ... to be able to step away from my computer,
to be able to have fun, to be able to get in the wilderness. I love being in the wilderness. I
love being in the wilderness for extended trips, so I don't a business where I have to be
on-call, or I have to be available all the time where I can't afford to take off two, or three,
or four weeks and be completely disconnected. Now, there aren't that many people that
value that. I think I'm bit of an oddball there. You have to figure out what do you value?
What do you want in your life? What do you want your life to look like?
I went through this process ... it was called Lifebook. It's a program called Lifebook.
It is a great, great program, and it had you walk through 12 areas of your life and define
what you want your life to look like in that area. When it comes to health, when it comes
to emotional health, physical health, mental health, when it comes to family relationships,
when it comes to parenting relationships, when it comes to your romantic relationship,
when it comes to your financial success, when it comes to your business success. And
every one of those areas, you sat down ... and they had a process to do this. You might
want to check it out. It's called Lifebook, MyLifebook is the website.
Sit down and design what you want in your life, and then when you make decisions
in your business, you should make them in alignment with what you want in your life.
You signed up to be an entrepreneur -- that means you're creating, you're working hard.
You might as well create what you want in your life. Create the life you want. Don't live by
someone else's rules -- you write the rules that you want. I mean, as long as they're legal
and ethical, write the rules that you want. Create the rules that you want that are gonna
give you the life that you want, and then build that business that gives you that.
